summary: This technical tutorial demonstrates how to extend .NET's AssemblyInfo file with custom attributes beyond the built-in ones. Atwood shows the complete implementation: defining custom attribute classes (AssemblyCompanyEmail and AssemblyCompanyUrl), applying them in AssemblyInfo, and retrieving all attributes via reflection into a NameValueCollection. The post follows up on his earlier argument that developers should properly populate their AssemblyInfo files, now showing how to go even further with customization.
key_points: Custom assembly attributes can be created by inheriting from System.Attribute and applying AttributeUsage targeting Assembly
The pattern for custom attributes is straightforward: a constructor that stores the value and a read-only property to expose it
Reflection's GetCustomAttributes method allows retrieval of both standard and custom attributes at runtime
A switch/case pattern can normalize attribute type names into friendly key-value pairs
money_quotes: To complement my previous post bemoaning the lack of respect for AssemblyInfo, I wanted to illustrate just how easy it is to add a few custom attributes to our AssemblyInfo file.
But I am sure there are other ways.
key_insight: Extending .NET assemblies with custom metadata attributes is straightforward and enables richer self-describing components through simple class inheritance and reflection.
